coredata.dc:title: Refractive index dependent optical property of carbon dots integrated luminescent solar concentrators
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Haiguang
coredata.dc:description: Colloidal Carbon dots (C-dots) have been used as promising emitters for luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) due to its high quantum yield, low-cost, non-toxic and simple synthetic method. While it is still very challenging to fabricate high-efficiency LSCs based on C-dots because of strong spectral overlap between the absorption and emission. It is still unclear how the refractive index of the C-dots layer affects the optical properties of LSCs. Here we used liquid junction LSCs as an example to investigate the refractive index effect. A large-area (∼100 cm2) LSC was fabricated by injecting C-dots/polyvinylpyrrolidone solution into a glass mold. The refractive index of the polymer solution can be well controlled in the range of 1.33–1.44 by simply enhancing the polymer concentration. The reabsorption energy loss and the optical efficiency of the LSCs are strongly dependent on the refractive index of the polymer solution. The LSC based on C-dots/polymer exhibits refractive index dependent optical efficiency, as high as ∼0.92% under sunlight illumination, which is 27% enhancement in efficiency over the LSC based on C-dots dispersed in methanol.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Carbon dots
Luminescence solar concentrators
Refractive index

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The theoretical optical efficiency of an LSC can be calculated as [1].
Tuning the refractive index of C-dots solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of (a) C-dots after Na+ treatment. (b) Typical absorption and PL spectra of Na + treated C-dots. (c) PL decay curve of C-dots dispersed in methanol. The inset TEM image is a single C-dot.

(a) Refractive index of C-dots dispersed in methanol with different concentrations of C-dots. (b) Refractive index of C-dots dispersed in PVP/methanol with different concentrations of PVP. The concentration of C-dots was 0.2 wt %.

(a) Scheme of a typical liquid-junction LSC configuration. (b) The light path for the liquid junction LSC. The escape cone is determined by the n of the C-dots mixture other than the glass substrate. (c–d) Photographs of the liquid junction LSC under one sun illumination (100 mW/cm2). Scale bar is 1 cm.

PL spectra (a) and integrated PL area (b) of C-dots in methanol solution with different concentrations of C-dots. The excitation wavelength is λ ex = 400 nm. Inset of (b) are the photographs of C-dots dispersed in methanol with C-dots concentration of 1.98 wt % (left), 0.66 wt % (middle), and 0.2 wt % (right) under UV light.
(a) PL spectra of the LSCs based on C-dots dispersed in PVP/methanol with the PVP concentration of 0% (a), 31 wt % (b) and 52 wt % (c). The concentration of C-dots is 0.2 wt % for all samples.
External optical efficiency of the LSC based on C-dots dispersed solution with different n. The concentration of C-dots is 0.2 wt % for all samples. The ηQY of C-dots is 40% in the simulation.
External optical efficiencies for LSCs based on C-dots under one sun illumination.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: C-dots were oil-soluble and embedded in PLMA polymer. During the measurement, a mirror was placed on the bottom of the LSC.

head.ce:title: Refractive index dependent optical property of carbon dots integrated luminescent solar concentrators

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics & State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, Qingdao University, No. 308 Ningxia Road, Qingdao, 266071, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics & State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Colloidal Carbon dots (C-dots) have been used as promising emitters for luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) due to its high quantum yield, low-cost, non-toxic and simple synthetic method. While it is still very challenging to fabricate high-efficiency LSCs based on C-dots because of strong spectral overlap between the absorption and emission. It is still unclear how the refractive index of the C-dots layer affects the optical properties of LSCs. Here we used liquid junction LSCs as an example to investigate the refractive index effect. A large-area (∼100 cm2) LSC was fabricated by injecting C-dots/polyvinylpyrrolidone solution into a glass mold. The refractive index of the polymer solution can be well controlled in the range of 1.33–1.44 by simply enhancing the polymer concentration. The reabsorption energy loss and the optical efficiency of the LSCs are strongly dependent on the refractive index of the polymer solution. The LSC based on C-dots/polymer exhibits refractive index dependent optical efficiency, as high as ∼0.92% under sunlight illumination, which is 27% enhancement in efficiency over the LSC based on C-dots dispersed in methanol.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have attracted a great attention due to their simple fabrication, adjustable transparency and low cost of electricity compared to that of silicon based photovoltaics (PVs) [1–4]. Typically, an LSC consists of an optical waveguide embedded with emissive fluorophores (e.g. dyes or polymers). Following absorption of sunlight, fluorophores re-emit photons, and these photons are guided towards the PVs positioned at their edges by total internal reflection (TIR). Then the concentrated emissions are converted to electricity by PVs [1]. The LSCs could be widely used for building integrated PVs (BIPVs) due to its light weight, the adjustable transparent nature and low-light intensity PV response [2,4–6].

Besides widely used organic dyes and polymers [7–9], various types of semiconducting materials have been used as light converters in solid-state LSCs, including quantum dots (QDs) [10,11], quantum wells [12,13], upconversion nanocrystals (NCs) [14] and perovskite NCs [15,16] etc. Although high external optical efficiency (defined as the number of photons emitted from the LSC edges over the total number of photons impinging on the LSC through the top surface) up to 1–3% of the LSC based on these semiconducting NCs (perovskite NCs or QDs) have been achieved [4,10,17], these NCs (e.g. CdSe/CdS [10], CdSe/CdPbS/CdS [11], PbS/CdS [18], Mn-CdSe/CdZnS [17], InP/ZnO [19], CuInS/ZnS [5], CuInSSe/ZnS [20]) usually contain heavy-metal (e.g. Cd or Pb) or environmentally sensitive elements (e.g. Cu), which limits these emitters used for further potential commercialization, unless these materials can be sealed very well during the LSC fabrication, use and further disposal.

Very recently, carbon dots (C-dots) [21–23] and Silicon dots [24] are emerging as excellent emitters for LSCs due to their high photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) (e.g. 53% [25] and 94% [26]), size/chemical composition tunable absorption spectrum (300–800 nm), low-cost, and toxic-metal-free composition. Compared to silicon dots (in which a very complicated approach was used), a very simple solvothermal approach has been used to produce large-quantity, high QY C-dots using earth-abundant, low-cost precursors [21–23,27,28]. In addition, these C-dots exhibit a broader absorption spectrum compared to silicon dots and they are very stable under the UV or sunlight [15,23,29]. Recently, C-dots have been used as efficient emitters in LSCs [21–23]. Zhou et al. have used two types of C-dots with different absorption range for tandem LSCs, achieving an external optical efficiency of 1.1% (LSC dimensions: 10 × 10 cm2) [23]. Gong et al. fabricated C-dots based LSCs with a very high efficiency, reaching 3.94% (LSC dimensions: 2.5 × 1.6 cm2) [21]. While the current achieved efficiency is still lower than that of LSCs based on dyes or inorganic emitters with similar active area, either due to their narrow absorption spectrum, or the spectral overlap of absorption and emission which often leads to reabsorption energy loss [1,7,10,14,19,30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The theoretical optical efficiency of an LSC can be calculated as [1].
ce:section.ce:para:
(1)
η
o p t
=
(
1 − R
)
P
T I R
⋅
η
A b s
⋅
η
Q Y
⋅
η
S t o k e s
⋅
η
H o s t
⋅
η
T I R
⋅
η
S e l f
where R is the reflection of light from the LSC surface, P TIR is the TIR efficiency, η Abs is the fraction of absorbed sunlight, η QY is the QY of emitters, η Stokes is the energy lost due to heat generation, η Host is the transport efficiency of the wave‐guided photons through the waveguide, η TIR is the reflection efficiency of the waveguide determined by the smoothness of the waveguide surface, and η Self is related to self-absorption due to the spectral overlap of absorption and emission. Usually, energy losses from re-absorption of the emitters (η Self) occur repetitively, because photons travelling within the waveguide can be absorbed by other emitters and re-emitted with similar energy band edges multiple times before reaching an edge. In practical LSC implementations, even very small overlap between absorption and emission spectra results in strong energy losses [1]. Thus, recent research on LSCs is still focusing on improving the optical efficiency by (i) decreasing the spectral overlap of emission/absorption of C-dots through structure engineering or surface treatment; (ii) improving the light absorption up to near infrared range by N doping; (iii) enhancing the refractive index (n) of the waveguide in order to reduce the energy loss due to the light escape; (ⅳ) increasing the QY of C-dots etc. In fact, most of factors are strongly dependent on the n of the waveguide (e.g. P TIR, and η Self) [4]. To the best of our knowledge, there is still no report to investigate the effect of n on the optical properties of LSCs based on C-dots.

Here we investigate the n dependent optical properties in LSCs using C-dots as light converter. The Na+ treated C-dots can absorb the sunlight in the broad UV and visible region (300–600 nm) and have a QY of 40%. The C-dots dispersed in methanol/pPolyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) was injected to glass container for liquid junction LSC fabrication. We are able to adjust the n of C-dots solution by tuning the PVP concentration. We found that the optical properties of LSCs are strongly dependent on the n of C-dots solution. With high n of the C-dots solution, the LSC has less reabsorption energy loss and higher optical efficiency. These results provide deep understanding for the optical property of LSCs and possible way to optimize the efficiency of LSCs based on C-dots.
PVP (MW: 50000), citric acid, urea, dimethylformamide, NaOH, hexane and methanol were obtained from Sigma Inc. All chemicals were used as purchased.

C-dots were prepared via a hydrothermal approach [23]. Typically, citric acid (1 g) and urea (2 g) were dissolved in dimethylformamide (10 mL). After the citric acid and urea were completely dissolved in the dimethylformamide, the mixture was transferred into an autoclave and reacted for 6 h at 160 °C. The product was precipitated by adding 50 mL hexane into the C-dots mixture. The precipitates were redispersed in 10 mL methanol and stored at −20 °C for 24 h. The mixture was centrifuged at 8000 r.p.m. for 10 min and the supernatant was collected. The product was mixed with 20 mL NaOH aqueous solution (50 mg/mL), and the mixture was stirred for 1 min. The product was then precipitated by adding hexane. Finally the mixture was redispersed in methanol and was transferred into dialysis bags with a molecular weight of 3000 Da for 1 h. The C-dots/methanol solution inside the dialysis bag was collected for further characterizations and use in fabricating LSCs.

The C-dots were dispersed in different concentration of PVP polymer/methanol solution with a fixed C-dots concentration (0.2 wt%). The solution was transferred into a glass container with dimensions of 10 × 10 × 1 cm3. The thickness of the glass slide is 0.4 cm. The C-dots were also dispersed in methanol with tunable C-dots concentrations.

The structural characterization of the C-dots was carried out using a JEOL JEM2100F transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Absorption spectra were acquired with a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) with a scan speed of 600 nm per minute. Fluorescence spectra were acquired using an FLS980 instrument (Edinburgh). The PL lifetime of the C-dots in methanol was measured using a pulsed laser diode of 350 nm and Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC) mode. QYs of C-dots were measured by using Rhodamine 6G as a reference. The n of C-dots/methanol solution or C-dots/polymer solution was measured using a WAY-2S Albert refractometer (INEA). The external optical efficiency of the LSCs was measured by using a solar simulator at AM 1.5G (100 mW cm2, Newport). The power meter (Newport Model 843-R) was directly coupled on the top side of the LSC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tuning the refractive index of C-dots solution

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to solid-state LSCs, liquid-junction LSCs have been used for solar energy conversion due to their several advantages, including the simple fabrication and low-cost, the easily control of the concentration and type of solvents and the easily variation of the geometry of the containers [31,32]. In this study, we used a liquid junction LSC to study the effect of n on the optical properties of LSCs based on C-dots. For the solution preparation, C-dots were dispersed in methanol (or PVP polymer), then sonicated for 10 min to exclude the bubbles until a clear solution was formed. The mixture was injected into a glass mold for the LSC fabrication. The lateral size of glass is 10 × 10 cm2 and the thickness of the glass is 4 mm. The thickness between two glasses is around 2 mm. Under the simulated sunlight illumination (100 mW/cm2), a clear yellow colour was seen in the LSC for both glass and solution, indicating the emission also travels inside the glass due to the refraction (Fig. 3 c) [33]. A gradient colour change was clearly seen in the side edge of the LSC because of the reabsorption of C-dots, which leads to red-shift (or double peak) of emission, when the sunlight was illuminated at the bottom of the LSC (Fig. 3d).

In order to study the effect of n on the optical properties of the LSCs, we keep the glass container same for all of the measurements. For the PL spectra measurement, we focused the excitation light on the different part of the LSC (light spot of 4 mm2), and detected the emission of LSCs on one of the edges [10,23]. As the reabsorption energy loss is strongly dependent on the C-dots concentration, we first investigated the effect of C-dots concentration on the optical properties of LSCs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the C-dots have a typical single emisison peak of ∼540 nm with the C-dots concentration less than 0.18 wt % in methanol. A clear PL shoulder at the longer wavelength began to appear with the C-dots concentration of 0.34 wt %. With further increase of the C-dots concentration (0.66 wt %), double peaks appeared. When the C-dots concentration reaches 1.98 wt %, only one peak was presented in the PL spetrum located at ∼620 nm.

The strong PL evolution with the increasing concentration of C-dots is due to the strong self-reabsortion of C-dots [23]. As the spectral overlap of absorption and PL is significant in the range of 450–550 nm (Fig. 1b), when the C-dots concentration is significant high, the emisison in the spectral overlap range will reabsorb by other C-dots, leading to either the decrease of PL intensity (Fig. 4a and b) or the appearance of double peak, even strongly red-shift of the PL peak. This phenomenon is consistent with the colour change in C-dots methanol solution with different C-dots concentrations (Fig. 4b, inset). For example, in the left photograph, the C-dots exhibit a strong red-colour, consistent with its PL spetrum, with a single PL peak of ∼620 nm. As shown in Fig. S1, the emission peak positions shift to the red as the increase of the excitation wavelength. The reabsorbed photons due to the spectral overlap can excite the C-dots, leading to the significant red-shift of the PL peak. To investigate the effect the n on the optical properties of LSCs, we should avoid the effect of C-dots concentration on the reabsorption. Thus in the following, we set the concentration of C-dots as 0.2 wt % as there is no strong self-absorption of C-dots. In this way, any of PL spectrum evolution will mainly depend on the n of the C-dots solution.

As shown in Fig. 5 a, with the increase of n, a typical double peak appears when the distance between the excitation spot on the LSC and the detector is 5 cm. While with an identical carbon concentration and measurement condition, the LSC with 31 wt % PVP (n = 1.3757) only shows a slight PL shoulder with a red-shift of the PL peak, and the LSC with 52 wt % PVP (n = 1.4438) exhibits only a slight PL width variation (Fig. 5b and c). As the PL spectral evolution is determined by the reabsorption, we can conclude that with the increase of n, the energy loss due to the reabsorption decreases. To further understand this phenomenon, we calculated the first-generation emission travelling distance inside the solution as the reabsorption does not happen inside the glass. Because the n of C-dots solution (methanol or PVP polymer) is lower than that of glass substrate (n = 1.5100), after absorbing the sunlight, the emissions of C-dots cannot travel inside the solution by TIR. Instead, as shown in Fig. 3b, on the interface of solution/glass, part of the emission is reflected to the solution, and others travel inside the glass by refraction. Then some of the emissions escape the waveguide though the escape cone on the interface of glass and air. As the n of glass is higher than the C-dots solution, the critical angle of the LSC will be determined by the critical angle of the solution layer. The critical angle (θ) from liquid to glass can be calculated by equation (2) [1].
(2)
θ = arcsin
( 1 / n )

The θ ranges from 43.8° to 48.7° with the decrease value of n (1.44 → 1.33). Assuming the travelling emission not absorbed by other emitters, the emission travelling distance inside the liquid waveguide can be calculated based on equation (3):
(3)
L =
L
lsc
Sinθ + 4 cosθ ⋅ tanθc
where L is the light travelling distance inside the liquid waveguide, Llsc is the length of the LSC, and θc is the critical angle on the interface of glass and air. As the sinθ equals to 1/n and for the glass, the sinθc equals to 1/1.5100, thus equation (3) equals to equation (4):
(4)
L =
L
lsc
1 / n + 3.54 / n ⋅
n
2
− 1

Based on equation (4), with the increase of n, L decreases. In detail, when the n increases from 1.33 to 1.44, the L decreases from 0.35Llsc to 0.3Llsc. As the reabsorption energy loss is proportional to L, the increase of n can limit the reabsorption of C-dots, leading to less PL spectra variation. This result also explains the presence of double emission in the LSC with lower n.

As shown in Fig. 3c, the LSCs (dimension: 10 × 10 × 1 cm3) were illuminated to its surface by an AM 1.5G solar simulator (100 mW/cm2). The geometrical gain factor (G) is 2.5. G is defined as the ratio of the area of the top of the LSC and the area of the edge of the LSC coupled with PV cell [10]. An optical power meter was coupled with the top edge of the LSC and measured the emitted light intensity. The LSC based on C-dots/methanol shows η of 0.67% (Table 1 ). The LSC based on C-dots/PVP shows η as high as 0.92% under one sun illumination (Table 1). In fact, with the increase of PVP concentration, the η gradually enhances (Table 1). The highest η reported in this work is comparable to the value reported of C-dots integrated LSCs in the literature considering the dimension of the LSCs (Table 1) [21,23].

The external optical efficiency of the LSCs can be expressed as [34].

In which α is the absorption coefficient [calculated as
α = ln

, where d is the effective length (2 mm C-dots solution) and A the absorption of the LSC],

is the Sun irradiance.

R can be calculated as follows [1].
(7)
R =
(

− n
)

+ n
)
2

R at the interface of glass and air is 4% as the nglass is 1.50 and nair is 1. R at the interface of glass and the mixture of the glass and the C-dots mixture is in the range of 0.36–0.44%, depending on the refractive index of the mixture.

A spectrally averaged internal efficiency (ηinternal) over the PL emission of the QDs was calculated as [34].
(8)
η
i n t e r n a l
=

Q Y
P
T I R
1 + β α

lsc

η

T I R
)
S
P L

)
d λ
∫
0

S
P L
(
λ
)
d λ
in which
S
P L
( λ )
is the PL emission spectrum; β is a numerical value fixed to 1.4 and Llsc is the length of the LSC. Assuming an isotropic emission, P TIR is defined by the escape cone identified by the critical angle θ of the solution/glass interface [1].
(9)
P
T I R
=
1 −
(
n
a i r
n
)
2
With the increase of n from 1.33 to 1.44, the P TIR increases from 65% to 72%, indicating the enhancement of the emission collection efficiency.

With the C-dots concentration of 0.3 wt %, the obtained ηAbs is around 4.2%. As shown in Fig. 6 , the higher n leads to higher external optical efficiency. This trend is consistent to the optical efficiency we measured for the liquid junction LSCs. In fact, in this geometry, once the n (>1.5) is higher than the glass, some fraction of emission will travel inside the solution under the TIR, which leads to significant reabsorption energy loss [10]. As the reabsorption energy loss is still the dominant factor to limit the optical efficiency of the LSCs based on C-dots, it is still significant to decrease the spectral overlap of the absorption and emission by either ion doping or surface modification [21,22,33]. Instead of solid-state structure, the LSCs may use liquid matrix or liquid crystalline matrix as efficient waveguide [31–33,35,36–38]. The as-fabricated liquid junction LSC is very stable regarding the colloidal stability of C-dots in the glass container and the optical efficiency of the LSC.

In conclusion, we demonstrated the n dependent optical properties in liquid junction LSCs using C-dots as emitter. The n of the C-dots solution can be well controlled by adding PVP polymer with the range of 1.33–1.44. With the increase of n, the reabsorption energy loss largely decreases. In addition, the energy loss due to the escape cone can also be decreased due to the decrease of critical angle with the increase of n. Finally, with the increase of n, the external optical efficiency of the LSC can be improved. The as-fabricated liquid-junction device based on C-dots/PVP solution exhibits an external optical efficiency of ∼0.92% under sunlight illumination (100 mW/cm2) which is 27% enhancement in efficiency over LSC based on C-dots dispersed in methanol. These results conclude that the optical properties of C-dots based LSCs strongly depend on the n of the solution. Future research direction may focus on fabrication LSCs by using polymer with optimized n.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: H. Zhao acknowledges the start funding support from Qingdao University and the funding from the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2018MB001).
